compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Member States shall ensure the confidentiality of communications and the related traffic data by means of a public communications network and publicly available electronic communications services. Listening, tapping, storage or other kinds of interception or surveillance of communications and the related traffic data by persons other than users is prohibited without the consent of the users concerned, except when legally authorised.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Italian DPA fined a telecoms provider EUR 800,000 for failing to ensure confidentiality of subscriber communications (2019).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: The storing of information, or the gaining of access to information already stored, in the terminal equipment of a subscriber or user is only allowed on condition that the subscriber or user concerned has given his or her consent, having been provided with clear and comprehensive information about the purposes of the processing. This covers cookies and similar tracking technologies. An exception exists for cookies strictly necessary for the provision of a service explicitly requested by the user.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: CNIL fined Google EUR 150 million and Facebook EUR 60 million for making cookie refusal more difficult than acceptance (2022).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Traffic data relating to subscribers and users processed and stored by the provider of a public communications network or publicly available electronic communications service must be erased or made anonymous when it is no longer needed for the purpose of the transmission of a communication, without prejudice to lawful retention requirements.
Traffic data necessary for subscriber billing and interconnection payments may be processed but only up to the end of the period during which the bill may lawfully be challenged or payment pursued.

For the purpose of marketing electronic communications services or for the provision of value added services, the provider may process traffic data to the extent and for the duration necessary for such services or marketing, only if the subscriber or user has given prior consent. Users or subscribers shall be given the possibility to withdraw consent at any time.

Where location data other than traffic data, relating to users or subscribers of public communications networks or publicly available electronic communications services, can be processed, such data may only be processed when they are made anonymous, or with the consent of the users or subscribers to the extent and for the duration necessary for the provision of a value added service.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Spanish DPA fined a company EUR 60,000 for processing employee location data from company phones without proper consent under ePrivacy rules (2021).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Where consent has been obtained for processing of location data other than traffic data, the user or subscriber must be given the possibility to temporarily refuse the processing of such data for each connection or each transmission of a communication using a simple means and free of charge.

The use of automated calling and communication systems without human intervention (automatic calling machines), facsimile machines (fax) or electronic mail for the purposes of direct marketing may be allowed only in respect of subscribers or users who have given their prior consent (opt-in requirement for electronic direct marketing).
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ICO (UK, pre-Brexit) fined companies multiple times for unsolicited marketing emails and texts without valid consent, with fines up to GBP 500,000.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: A natural or legal person who obtains electronic contact details for electronic mail in the context of the sale of a product or service (soft opt-in) may use those details for direct marketing of its own similar products or services provided that customers are clearly and distinctly given the opportunity to object, free of charge and in an easy manner, to such use at the time of collection and on each subsequent message.

Member States shall take appropriate measures to ensure that unsolicited communications for the purposes of direct marketing are not allowed either without the consent of the subscribers concerned or in respect of subscribers who do not wish to receive these communications. The choice between opt-in and opt-out mechanisms shall be determined by national legislation.

The practice of sending electronic mail for purposes of direct marketing disguising or concealing the identity of the sender on whose behalf the communication is made, or without a valid address to which the recipient may send a request that such communications cease, shall be prohibited.

The provider of a publicly available electronic communications service must take appropriate technical and organisational measures to safeguard security of its services, if necessary in conjunction with the provider of the public communications network with respect to network security. Having regard to the state of the art and the cost of their implementation, these measures shall ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk presented.

In case of a particular risk of a breach of the security of the network, the provider of a publicly available electronic communications service must inform the subscribers concerning such risk and, where the risk lies outside the scope of the measures to be taken by the service provider, of any possible remedies, including an indication of the likely costs involved.

In the case of a personal data breach, the provider of publicly available electronic communications services shall, without undue delay, notify the personal data breach to the competent national authority. When the personal data breach is likely to adversely affect the personal data or privacy of a subscriber or individual, the provider shall also notify the subscriber or individual of the breach without undue delay.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Several EU DPAs have fined telecoms providers for late breach notification under the ePrivacy framework, including Romanian DPA fining a telecoms provider EUR 14,000 (2020).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Where required for implementing the ePrivacy Directive, the processing of personal data by service providers must comply with the applicable data protection directive/regulation (now GDPR). This provision ensures that ePrivacy obligations in contracts are read alongside and consistent with GDPR data processing requirements.

Processing of personal data may be carried out upon the provision of consent by the data subject. Consent must be provided in writing or by another means that demonstrates the manifestation of the will of the data subject. Contracts must specify the lawful basis and include provisions for obtaining and recording valid consent where consent is relied upon.

Processing of personal data may be carried out when necessary for the execution of a contract or preliminary procedures related to a contract to which the data subject is a party, at the request of the data subject. Contracts relying on this basis must clearly define the processing activities that are necessary for performance.

Processing of personal data may be carried out when necessary to serve the legitimate interests of the controller or a third party, except in the case where the fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject that require protection of personal data prevail. A legitimate interests assessment must be documented and referenced in any related contract.

Consent must refer to specific purposes and generic authorisations for the processing of personal data shall be null and void. When consent is provided in a written declaration, it must be in a clause that is highlighted and distinguished from other contractual clauses.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD issued guidance reinforcing that blanket consent clauses buried in terms of service are not valid under LGPD (2022).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Consent may be revoked at any time by means of an express manifestation of the data subject, through a free and facilitated procedure, and the processing carried out prior to revocation is ratified. Contracts must include a mechanism and procedure for consent withdrawal.

The data subject has the right to facilitated access to information regarding the processing of their data, including the specific purpose of the processing, the form and duration of the processing, and the identification and contact information of the controller. Contracts must require the provision of this information.

The data subject has the right to obtain from the controller, at any time and upon request, confirmation of the existence of processing. This right must be honoured within a reasonable time frame. Contracts involving data processing must obligate processors to assist controllers in responding to data subject access requests.

The data subject has the right to access the data held by the controller. Processors must be contractually obligated to assist the controller in providing this access, including making available all personal data processed on the controller's behalf.

The data subject has the right to the anonymisation, blocking or deletion of unnecessary data, excessive data, or data processed in non-compliance with the provisions of the LGPD. Contracts must include provisions enabling the controller to instruct the processor to anonymise, block, or delete data in response to data subject requests.

The data subject has the right to data portability to another service provider or product supplier, upon express request, in accordance with the regulations of the national authority, subject to commercial and industrial secrets. Contracts should address portability obligations and data format requirements.

The international transfer of personal data is only permitted in specified circumstances, including when the country or international organisation provides an adequate degree of protection, when the controller offers and proves guarantees of compliance through contractual clauses (standard or specific), global corporate rules, regularly issued stamps or certifications, or the data subject has provided specific and highlighted consent for the transfer.

International transfer of personal data is permitted when the controller offers and proves guarantees of compliance with the principles, data subject rights and data protection regime provided for in the LGPD, in the form of specific contractual clauses for a given transfer, standard contractual clauses, or binding corporate rules.

The controller and the processor shall maintain records of personal data processing operations that they carry out, especially when based on legitimate interest. Contracts must require processors to maintain and provide access to processing records when requested by the controller or ANPD.

The national authority may request the controller to prepare a data protection impact assessment (DPIA), including sensitive data, with description of the types of data collected, the methodology used, and the security measures and mechanisms to mitigate risk. Contracts should require the processor to cooperate with DPIA preparation.

The processor shall carry out the processing of personal data according to the instructions provided by the controller, who shall verify the compliance of the processing with instructions and applicable legal standards. This establishes the processor's duty of obedience and the controller's duty of oversight.

The controller shall appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO, called encarregado in the LGPD) who shall accept complaints and communications from data subjects, receive communications from ANPD, guide employees and contractors, and execute other duties determined by the controller or established in complementary rules. The DPO's identity and contact information must be publicly disclosed.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD published resolution CD/ANPD No. 18/2024 detailing the role and responsibilities of the DPO, including when one must be appointed.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Processing agents shall adopt security, technical and administrative measures able to protect personal data from unauthorised access and from accidental or unlawful situations of destruction, loss, alteration, communication, or any form of improper or unlawful processing. Contracts must specify the security measures the processor will implement.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD sanctioned Telekall Infoservice with a warning and fine for insufficient security measures that led to the disclosure of personal data (2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Processing agents or any other person who intervenes in one of the phases of the processing is obligated to guarantee the security of the information provided for in the LGPD in relation to the personal data, even after the end of the processing. Post-termination confidentiality and security obligations must be addressed in contracts.

The controller must communicate to the national authority (ANPD) and to the data subject the occurrence of a security incident that may create risk or relevant damage to the data subjects. The communication must be made within a reasonable time period as defined by ANPD, and must describe the nature of the affected personal data, the technical and security measures used, the risks related to the incident, and the measures taken to reverse or mitigate the effects.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD issued multiple notifications to controllers for delays in communicating data breaches, and published Regulation CD/ANPD No. 15/2024 establishing 3-business-day notification deadlines.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: The controller or processor who, while carrying out processing of personal data, causes patrimonial, moral, individual or collective damage to others, in violation of data protection legislation, is obliged to repair it. The processor is jointly and severally liable when it fails to comply with the obligations of the data protection legislation or when it has not followed the lawful instructions of the controller. Contracts should address liability allocation between controller and processor.

The processing of personal data shall be deemed irregular when it does not comply with the legislation or when it does not provide the security that the data subject can expect, considering the circumstances relevant to the processing such as the manner in which it is carried out, the result and the risks reasonably expected, and the techniques available at the time of the processing.

Controllers and processors, individually or through associations, may formulate rules of good practices and governance that establish conditions for the organisation, regime of operation, procedures including complaints and petitions by data subjects, applicable standards of security, technical standards, specific obligations, educational actions, internal oversight mechanisms, risk mitigation mechanisms, and other aspects related to the processing of personal data. Contracts may reference adherence to such codes of conduct.

Personal data processing activities must observe good faith and the following principles: purpose (processing for legitimate, specific, and explicit purposes disclosed to the data subject), adequacy (compatibility with the stated purposes), necessity (limitation to the minimum necessary), free access (guarantee of easy and free consultation), quality of data (guarantee of accuracy), transparency, security, prevention, non-discrimination, and accountability and demonstration. Contracts must require processors to adhere to these principles and demonstrate compliance.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD referenced Article 6 principles in its first enforcement action against Telekall Infoservice, emphasising the necessity and purpose limitation principles (2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Processing agents that commit infractions of the LGPD are subject to administrative sanctions by the ANPD, including warnings, simple fines of up to 2% of the revenue of a private legal entity in Brazil (capped at BRL 50 million per infraction), daily fines, publicisation of the infraction, blocking or deletion of personal data, and suspension or prohibition of processing activities. Contracts should include indemnification provisions for regulatory fines.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: ANPD applied its first administrative fine (BRL 14,400) against Telekall Infoservice for processing personal data without a lawful basis and for failing to appoint a DPO (2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Processing of sensitive personal data may only take place when the data subject or legal guardian provides specific and highlighted consent, for specific purposes, or, without consent, in exhaustively listed scenarios such as compliance with legal obligations, execution of public policies, health protection, or prevention of fraud. Contracts involving sensitive data must specify the lawful basis and include additional safeguards.

Personal data shall be eliminated after the end of the processing period, within the scope and technical limits of the activities, with authorised retention for compliance with legal or regulatory obligations, study and research (with anonymisation guaranteed), transfer to third parties (compliant with LGPD requirements), or exclusive use by the controller (with anonymisation). Contracts must specify data retention periods and post-termination deletion procedures.

Where consent is the lawful basis for processing personal information, consent must be given by the individual on a voluntary and explicit basis with full knowledge of the matter. Where laws or administrative regulations provide that separate consent or written consent is required for the processing of personal information, such provisions shall prevail. Contracts must describe how consent is obtained and managed.

Where consent is the basis for processing personal information, the consent must be specific and clear. When the purpose, method, or scope of processing changes, consent must be obtained again from the individual. Contracts must include procedures for re-obtaining consent when processing activities change.

Where consent of the individual is the lawful basis for personal information processing, the individual has the right to withdraw consent. Personal information handlers shall provide a convenient method for the individual to withdraw consent. Withdrawal of consent does not affect the lawfulness of processing carried out before withdrawal. Contracts should include provisions for managing consent withdrawals.

Personal information handlers that entrust the handling of personal information to other parties shall agree with the entrusted party on the purposes, time limits, handling methods, types of personal information, protection measures, and the rights and obligations of both parties, and shall supervise the entrusted party's handling activities. The entrusted party may not sub-entrust to any other party without the consent of the personal information handler.

Where a personal information handler needs to transfer personal information due to merger, division, dissolution, declaration of bankruptcy, or similar reasons, it shall inform the individual of the receiving party's name or identity and contact information. The receiving party shall continue to fulfil the obligations of the personal information handler. If the receiving party changes the original purpose or method of processing, it must re-obtain consent.

Personal information handlers that use personal information for automated decision-making shall ensure the transparency and fairness of the result, and may not impose unreasonable differential treatment on individuals in transaction conditions such as price. Where automated decision-making is used to push information or conduct commercial marketing to individuals, a convenient method to refuse must be provided. Contracts involving automated decisions must include transparency and non-discrimination clauses.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: CAC investigated and fined several technology companies for algorithmic recommendation systems that failed to meet transparency requirements under PIPL and related algorithm regulations (2022-2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Personal information handlers may not disclose personal information they have processed, except where the individual provides separate consent. Contracts must specify circumstances under which personal information may be disclosed and require prior individual consent for any disclosure.

Handling of sensitive personal information (including biometric data, religious beliefs, specified identity, medical health, financial accounts, and location tracking, as well as any personal information of minors under 14) is only permitted where there is a specific purpose and sufficient necessity, and strict protection measures are adopted. Separate consent of the individual must be obtained. Contracts involving sensitive personal information must include enhanced protection measures.

Where the handling of sensitive personal information requires the individual's consent, separate consent must be obtained from the individual. Where laws or administrative regulations provide that written consent must be obtained, such provisions shall prevail.

Where a personal information handler truly needs to provide personal information outside the borders of the People's Republic of China due to business needs, it shall meet at least one of the following conditions: passing a security assessment organised by the CAC, obtaining personal information protection certification from a specialised institution, entering into a standard contract formulated by the CAC with the overseas recipient, or meeting other conditions provided by laws, administrative regulations, or the CAC.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: CAC published Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) for cross-border personal information transfers effective June 2023, mandating their use for qualifying transfers.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Where personal information is to be provided outside the borders of the People's Republic of China, the personal information handler must inform the individual of the overseas recipient's name, contact information, purpose of processing, methods of processing, types of personal information involved, and procedures for the individual to exercise rights. Separate consent must be obtained from the individual.

Critical information infrastructure operators and personal information handlers processing personal information reaching the volume thresholds prescribed by the CAC must store personal information collected and generated within the territory of the People's Republic of China domestically. Where it is truly necessary to provide such information outside the borders, a security assessment organised by the CAC must be passed.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Didi Global was fined CNY 8.026 billion (approximately USD 1.19 billion) for violations including illegal cross-border data transfers and failure to conduct security assessments (2022).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Personal information handlers shall, in accordance with the purpose and method of processing, the type of personal information, and the impact on individuals' rights and interests, adopt measures including formulating internal management systems and operating procedures, implementing classified management of personal information, adopting corresponding technical security measures such as encryption and de-identification, reasonably determining operational authority, and regularly conducting security education and training for employees.

Personal information handlers shall regularly conduct compliance audits of their handling of personal information and compliance with laws and administrative regulations. Contracts should require processors to participate in and facilitate compliance audits conducted by or on behalf of the personal information handler.

Personal information handlers must conduct a personal information protection impact assessment (PIPIA) before processing in high-risk scenarios, including: processing sensitive personal information, using personal information for automated decision-making, entrusting processing or providing personal information to other handlers, providing personal information overseas, or other activities with a major impact on individuals. The assessment must include whether the purpose and method are lawful, the impact on rights and interests, and whether protective measures are adequate.

Personal information protection impact assessment reports and handling status records shall be retained for at least three years. Contracts must include record-keeping obligations and specify minimum retention periods for impact assessment documentation and processing records.

Where a personal information security incident occurs or may occur, the personal information handler shall immediately take remedial measures and notify the departments performing personal information protection duties and the individuals. The notification must include the types and causes of the personal information leaked, the harm that may be caused, the remedial measures adopted, and the measures the individual may take to mitigate harm.

Where violation of the PIPL is serious, the department performing personal information protection duties at or above the provincial level shall order correction, confiscate illegal income, and impose a fine of not more than CNY 50 million or not more than 5% of the prior year's revenue. The department may also order the suspension of related business activities, suspend business for rectification, or revoke related business permits or business licences. Responsible persons may be fined between CNY 100,000 and CNY 1 million and may be prohibited from serving as directors, supervisors, or senior managers for a specified period.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Didi Global was fined CNY 8.026 billion under PIPL and related regulations. Multiple apps have been removed from app stores for PIPL non-compliance (2022-2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Where a personal information handler ceases processing (due to dissolution, closure, revocation of licence, etc.), it shall promptly stop processing activities, notify individuals, and delete the personal information. If the personal information handler does not delete the information, the department performing personal information protection duties shall order deletion. Contracts must include post-termination data deletion obligations.

Financial institutions must provide customers with a clear and conspicuous privacy notice that describes the institution's privacy policies and practices, including the categories of personal information collected, the categories of persons to whom the information is disclosed, the institution's policies regarding protection of the confidentiality and security of nonpublic personal information, and the consumer's right to opt out of certain disclosures. This notice must be provided at the time of establishing a customer relationship and not less than annually thereafter.

Financial institutions must provide consumers with a reasonable opportunity to opt out of the disclosure of their nonpublic personal information to nonaffiliated third parties before such disclosure occurs. The opt-out notice must be clear and conspicuous, identify the categories of information that may be disclosed, identify the categories of third parties, and explain how the consumer may exercise the opt-out right. Contracts with third-party processors must incorporate restrictions reflecting consumer opt-out choices.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: FTC has brought multiple enforcement actions against financial institutions for failing to provide adequate opt-out notices.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Financial institutions must develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive information security program that contains administrative, technical, and physical safeguards appropriate to the institution's size and complexity, the nature and scope of its activities, and the sensitivity of customer information. The revised FTC Safeguards Rule (effective June 2023) requires specific measures including encryption, multi-factor authentication, a qualified individual to oversee the program, periodic risk assessments, and incident response planning. Contracts with service providers must require implementation of appropriate safeguards.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: FTC fined CafePress USD 500,000 for GLBA Safeguards Rule violations after a data breach exposed millions of customer records (2022). FTC ordered Chegg to implement a comprehensive security program for Safeguards Rule failures (2022).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Financial institutions must oversee service providers by taking reasonable steps to select and retain service providers that are capable of maintaining appropriate safeguards for the customer information at issue, requiring service providers by contract to implement and maintain such safeguards, and periodically assessing service providers based on the risk they present and the continued adequacy of their safeguards.

It is illegal to obtain or attempt to obtain customer information of a financial institution relating to another person by making a false, fictitious, or fraudulent statement or representation to the institution, by using forged, counterfeit, lost, or stolen documents, or by using other fraudulent means. Contracts with service providers should include representations against pretexting and social engineering attacks and obligations to implement controls to prevent fraudulent access to customer information.

A nonaffiliated third party that receives nonpublic personal information from a financial institution may not directly or through an affiliate disclose such information to any other person that is a nonaffiliated third party of both the financial institution and the third party recipient, unless such disclosure would be lawful if made directly by the financial institution. Contracts with third-party recipients must include reuse and redisclosure limitations.

The FTC has enforcement authority over financial institutions subject to GLBA requirements that are not regulated by another federal agency. The FTC may bring enforcement actions for unfair or deceptive practices related to privacy and safeguards. Contracts should include compliance representations and indemnification provisions for FTC enforcement consequences.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: FTC has brought enforcement actions resulting in comprehensive security orders against financial institutions including PayPal, Venmo, and various fintech companies.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Financial institutions must establish and implement a written incident response plan designed to promptly respond to and recover from any security event that materially affects the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of customer information. The plan must address the goals, internal processes, roles and responsibilities, communication procedures, documentation requirements, and post-incident analysis. Notification to the FTC is required when a security event affects the information of 500 or more consumers.

Financial institutions must encrypt all customer information held or transmitted in transit over external networks and at rest. Multi-factor authentication must be implemented for any individual accessing customer information systems. Contracts with service providers handling customer information must require the implementation of encryption and MFA as minimum technical safeguards.

Financial institutions must conduct a periodic written risk assessment that identifies reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to the security, confidentiality, and integrity of customer information, assesses the sufficiency of any safeguards in place, and is documented in a written assessment. Contracts with service providers should require cooperation with and participation in risk assessments relevant to the services provided.

Principal executive officers and principal financial officers must certify in each annual and quarterly report that they are responsible for establishing and maintaining internal controls, have designed such internal controls to ensure that material information is made known to them, have evaluated the effectiveness of internal controls within 90 days prior to the report, and have presented their conclusions about the effectiveness of internal controls. Contracts with service providers whose systems affect financial reporting must include provisions for internal control compliance and reporting.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: SEC has brought enforcement actions against officers who certified financial reports with inadequate internal controls, resulting in personal fines and trading suspensions.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Each annual report must contain an internal control report that states management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure and procedures for financial reporting, and contains an assessment of the effectiveness of the internal control structure and procedures as of the end of the fiscal year. External auditors must attest to and report on the assessment. Service providers processing financial data must contractually support SOX compliance through appropriate controls, access for audit, and timely reporting of control deficiencies.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Multiple companies have faced SEC enforcement for material weaknesses in internal controls over financial reporting, including PCAOB sanctions against audit firms.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Any person who knowingly alters, destroys, mutilates, conceals, covers up, falsifies, or makes a false entry in any record, document, or tangible object with the intent to impede, obstruct, or influence the investigation or proper administration of any matter within the jurisdiction of any federal department or agency, or in relation to or contemplation of any such matter or case, shall be fined and/or imprisoned for not more than 20 years. Audit workpapers and related documents must be retained for not less than 7 years. Contracts must include document and records retention clauses aligned with SOX requirements.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: Arthur Andersen was convicted under this provision for shredding documents related to the Enron audit, leading to the firm's dissolution.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: No publicly traded company or any officer, employee, contractor, subcontractor, or agent of such company may discharge, demote, suspend, threaten, harass, or in any other manner discriminate against an employee who provides information or assists in an investigation regarding conduct that the employee reasonably believes constitutes a violation of federal securities laws, SEC rules, or any provision of federal law relating to fraud against shareholders. Contracts with service providers should include whistleblower protection provisions and non-retaliation clauses.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: SEC and DOL have awarded whistleblowers millions in compensation and back pay for retaliation, with awards under the SEC whistleblower program exceeding USD 1 billion total.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Each issuer reporting under Section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 shall disclose to the public on a rapid and current basis such additional information concerning material changes in the financial condition or operations of the issuer, in plain English, as the Commission determines, by rule, is necessary or useful for the protection of investors and in the public interest. Contracts should include material event notification obligations to support real-time disclosure requirements.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: SEC has brought enforcement actions for delayed disclosure of material cybersecurity incidents, including against SolarWinds and its CISO (2023).

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: An electronic signature shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an electronic form or that it does not meet the requirements for qualified electronic signatures. A qualified electronic signature shall have the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature. Contracts should specify the acceptable levels of electronic signatures (simple, advanced, or qualified) and the legal effect attributed to each level within the jurisdiction of the contract.

An advanced electronic signature shall meet the following requirements: it is uniquely linked to the signatory, it is capable of identifying the signatory, it is created using electronic signature creation data that the signatory can use under their sole control with a high level of confidence, and it is linked to the data signed therewith in such a way that any subsequent change in the data is detectable. Contracts requiring advanced electronic signatures must verify the signature solution meets these criteria.

Member States shall issue European Digital Identity Wallets that enable users to securely store, manage, and present identity data, electronic attestations of attributes, and electronic signatures in a user-controlled manner. The wallets must ensure a high level of security and interoperability across Member States. Relying parties that accept digital identity wallets must contractually ensure they do not request more data than necessary, maintain privacy by design, and ensure data minimisation. Contracts for trust services involving wallet integration must specify data handling obligations.

Trust service providers shall take appropriate technical and organisational measures to manage the risks posed to the security of the trust services they provide. Having regard to the latest technological developments, those measures shall ensure that the level of security is commensurate with the degree of risk. In particular, measures shall be taken to prevent and minimise the impact of security incidents and to inform stakeholders of the adverse effects of any such incidents. Trust service contracts must include security requirements, breach notification, and audit provisions.
compliance_requirements.enforcement_examples: National supervisory bodies have audited qualified trust service providers and required remediation of security deficiencies, with several providers losing qualified status temporarily.

compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Qualified trust service providers shall be audited at least every 24 months by a conformity assessment body at their own expense. The audit shall confirm that the qualified trust service providers and the qualified trust services provided by them fulfil the requirements laid down in the Regulation. The report shall be submitted to the supervisory body within three working days. Contracts with qualified trust service providers must include audit cooperation obligations and provisions for maintaining qualified status.
